reference: GATE CS — Databases / Storage and Indexing

map.condition: Height / search cost of B+ tree with n leaves
Max keys in internal node of order d (2d definition)
Number of index blocks needed for ISAM index
Disk blocks required to hold all data records
Blocking factor given record and block sizes

formulas.name: B+ Tree Order (2d definition)
formulas.plain: Internal node holds between d and 2d keys; root holds 1 to 2d keys.
formulas.whenToUse: When the order is given as d and you need key/pointer bounds per node.

terms.means: order of the B+ tree (minimum occupancy)

formulas.trap: GATE sometimes defines order as the maximum number of pointers (= 2d+1), so read the problem statement carefully.

formulas.name: Pointers per Internal Node
formulas.plain: Internal node has between d+1 and 2d+1 child pointers.
formulas.whenToUse: When counting children/fan-out of internal nodes.
formulas.trap: Number of pointers = number of keys + 1 for internal nodes only; leaf nodes point to data records.

formulas.name: Leaf Node Key Fill
formulas.plain: Each leaf holds between ceil(d) and 2d keys (data pointers).
formulas.whenToUse: When computing minimum/maximum records stored in leaf nodes.
formulas.trap: Leaves do NOT have a right-pointing child pointer counted as a key; the last pointer links to the next leaf.

formulas.name: B+ Tree Height (Search Complexity)
formulas.plain: Height <= ceil(log_{d+1}(n)) + 1; search needs O(log_d n) I/Os.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many node accesses (disk I/Os) are needed to find a record.
formulas.trap: Use d+1 as the fan-out base, NOT d. Root may hold fewer than d keys, so actual height can be one less.

formulas.name: Minimum Keys in a B+ Tree of Height h
formulas.plain: Minimum keys = 1 (root) + 2 * (ceil(d/2)) * sum of branching factors over remaining levels.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked the minimum number of keys a B+ tree of given height can hold.
formulas.trap: This is rarely computed directly; GATE usually asks for node accesses or insertion/deletion steps.

formulas.name: Max Leaf Nodes for Height h
formulas.plain: Max leaves = (2d+1)^(h-1).
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding how many records a tree of height h can index.

terms.means: tree height (root at level 1, leaves at level h)

formulas.trap: Apply (2d+1) fan-out, not 2d; the +1 comes from pointers = keys + 1.

formulas.name: Leaf Split on Insertion
formulas.latex: \text{If leaf overflows (}> 2d\text{ keys): split into } \lceil (2d+1)/2 \rceil \text{ and } \lfloor (2d+1)/2 \rfloor
formulas.plain: On overflow push ceil((2d+1)/2) keys to left child and floor((2d+1)/2) to right; copy up the middle key to parent.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing an insertion that causes a leaf overflow.
formulas.trap: In B+ trees the split key is COPIED UP (not pushed up) to the parent; the key stays in the leaf. In B-trees it is PUSHED up.

formulas.name: Internal Node Split on Overflow
formulas.latex: \text{Internal overflow (}> 2d\text{ keys): push up middle key, left gets } d \text{ keys, right gets } d \text{ keys}
formulas.plain: On internal overflow: middle key is pushed up; left and right children each get d keys.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing a cascading split that reaches an internal node.
formulas.trap: The middle key is PUSHED (removed) from the internal node before going to parent; unlike the leaf split where it is copied.

formulas.name: Deletion: Underflow & Merge
formulas.latex: \text{Underflow if keys} < d; \quad \text{borrow from sibling or merge + delete parent separator}
formulas.plain: Underflow when keys < d. First try to borrow from a sibling; if not possible, merge two siblings and remove separator from parent.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing a deletion that leaves a node with fewer than d keys.
formulas.trap: After merging two leaves into one, the parent loses a pointer; this can cascade upward.

formulas.name: Dense vs Sparse Index
formulas.latex: \text{Dense: one entry per record} \quad \text{Sparse: one entry per block}
formulas.plain: Dense index: one index entry per data record. Sparse index: one entry per data block (requires sorted file).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which index type is used or how many index entries exist.
formulas.trap: Sparse index requires the data file to be sorted on the search key; dense index does not.

formulas.name: Primary vs Secondary Index
formulas.latex: \text{Primary: index on ordering key} \quad \text{Secondary: index on non-ordering field}
formulas.plain: Primary (clustered) index built on the field that defines physical order. Secondary index on any other field — must be dense.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which index type fits the query scenario.
formulas.trap: A primary index need NOT be on the primary key; it is on the ordering field. Secondary index is always dense.

formulas.name: Multi-level Index Access Cost
formulas.plain: Levels = ceil(log_{BF}(N)); total I/Os = levels + 1 (one for data block).
formulas.whenToUse: Computing number of disk accesses in a multi-level (ISAM) index structure.

terms.means: blocking factor of index (index entries per block)
number of index entries at lowest level

formulas.trap: Add 1 for the final data-block fetch; do not stop at the last index level.

formulas.name: ISAM: Number of Index Blocks
formulas.plain: Index blocks I = ceil(N / B_I), where N = total records and B_I = index entries per block.
formulas.whenToUse: Finding how many index blocks an ISAM index needs.

terms.means: number of index entries that fit in one block

formulas.trap: Index entry size = key size + block-pointer size; do not confuse with record size.

formulas.name: Blocking Factor
formulas.plain: BF = floor(Block Size / Record Size).
formulas.whenToUse: First step in any disk-storage computation — how many records fit in one block.
formulas.trap: Use floor (not ceil) because a partial record cannot be split across blocks (fixed-length records assumed).

formulas.name: Number of Data Blocks
formulas.plain: Data blocks D = ceil(N / BF).
formulas.whenToUse: Calculating how many physical blocks store all N records.
formulas.trap: Use ceil here (some blocks may be partially full); contrast with floor used in BF calculation.

formulas.name: Heap File Search Cost
formulas.plain: Average search in unsorted heap file = O(N) block accesses.
formulas.whenToUse: Baseline comparison when evaluating index benefit.
formulas.trap: Heap file is best for bulk inserts and full scans; terrible for point queries without an index.

formulas.name: Sorted File Binary Search
formulas.plain: Binary search on sorted file needs O(log2 N) block I/Os.
formulas.whenToUse: When the file is sorted on search key and no index exists.
formulas.trap: Inserts into sorted file are expensive (O(N)); ISAM or B+ tree preferred for mixed workloads.

formulas.name: ISAM (Indexed Sequential) Structure
formulas.latex: \text{ISAM: static B-tree-like structure; overflow chains degrade performance over time}
formulas.plain: ISAM uses a fixed tree-like index; new records go into overflow chains, which grow and slow searches.
formulas.whenToUse: When a question describes an ISAM or indexed-sequential file and asks about overflow or reorganization.
formulas.trap: Unlike B+ tree, ISAM does NOT rebalance; long overflow chains require periodic file reorganization.

formulas.name: Inverted File / Index
formulas.latex: \text{Inverted index: maps attribute value } \to \text{list of record IDs (best for secondary key search)}
formulas.plain: Inverted index maps each non-key value to the list of record addresses; optimal for secondary-attribute queries.
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks which file organization suits secondary-key or multi-attribute queries.
formulas.trap: Inverted index does not require the data file to be sorted; it stores posting lists per attribute value.

formulas.name: B-Tree: Keys in All Nodes
formulas.latex: \text{B-Tree: data pointers stored at internal nodes AND leaves}
formulas.plain: B-tree stores data pointers at both internal and leaf nodes; key may be found before reaching a leaf.
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing B-tree and B+ tree search cost or structure.
formulas.trap: B-tree internal nodes hold both key+data pointers, reducing fan-out; so B-tree is usually taller than B+ tree with same data.

formulas.name: B+ Tree: Leaf Linked List
formulas.latex: \text{B+ Tree leaves form a sorted linked list} \Rightarrow \text{range queries cost } O(\log_d n + k)
formulas.plain: B+ tree leaves are linked; range query cost = O(log_d n) to find start + O(k) to scan k results.
formulas.whenToUse: Range queries, order-by queries, full-table scans using the index.
formulas.trap: B-trees do NOT have leaf-level linked lists, making range queries more expensive (need to backtrack).
